%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Objective:** Understand how to create and manipulate vectors and matrices in MATLAB, and how to apply these tools to solve real engineering problems.

Section B.7-6 introduces MATLAB's core matrix operations — the building blocks for nearly every numerical computation in signals and systems. You will learn how to construct row vectors, column vectors, and full matrices using bracket notation and semicolons. You will also learn the transpose operator (`'`), which flips rows into columns, and colon indexing, which selects entire rows or columns at once. After this section, you will be able to represent a system of linear equations in matrix form \(Ax = y\) and manipulate its components confidently in MATLAB.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Fig. B.14](/figures/page-049-fig__b_14-1.png)
*Fig. B.14 shows a graphical comparison of two signals \(f(t)\) and \(h(t)\), illustrating the kind of vector-based data that MATLAB matrix operations are designed to store, index, and manipulate.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="comparison_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Three core MATLAB matrix operations: constructing a matrix with brackets and semicolons, transposing with `'`, and selecting an entire row or column with colon indexing.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777214428938-8892.png)

## Core Idea

The central relationship in B.7-6 is this: **MATLAB represents structured data as matrices, and three operations — construction, transposition, and indexing — give you full control over that structure.**

### CONSTRUCTION

Use square brackets to build a matrix. Spaces (or commas) separate elements within a row; semicolons separate rows:

```
A = [2 3; 4 5; 0 6]
```

This creates a \(3 \times 2\) matrix. Every row must have the same number of elements — mismatched lengths cause an error.

### TRANSPOSE

The single-quote operator `'` performs the **complex-conjugate transpose**. For a real vector, this simply flips a row vector into a column vector. For a complex vector, use `.'` for a plain transpose without conjugation.

### COLON INDEXING

The colon `:` selects all elements along one dimension. `B(2,:)` returns every element in row 2 of matrix \(B\).

### EXAM TRAP

> The `'` operator conjugates complex entries. If your vector is complex and you only want a geometric transpose, use `.'` instead.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Use `[... ; ...]` to build matrices — semicolons separate rows, spaces separate elements within a row.
- The `'` operator transposes and conjugates; use `.'` for plain transpose on complex vectors.
- Colon indexing `B(2,:)` selects an entire row or column in one clean expression.

*With matrix creation and indexing in hand, the next section applies these tools directly to solving systems of linear equations using MATLAB's backslash operator.*
